section.title: Israel Chosen and Idols Exposed
section.sectionRationale: This chapter continues the Book of Comfort's sustained argument against idolatry, moving from tender assurance that God will pour out His Spirit on Israel's descendants to one of Scripture's most vivid satires of idol-making, a craftsman using half a log for fuel and half to carve a god, before closing with the LORD's own joyful announcement that He has redeemed Jacob and blotted out its sin like a vanishing cloud.
section.thesis: The LORD who formed Israel from the womb and will pour out His Spirit on its descendants alone deserves worship, for no idol, carved by the same hands that use its other half for cooking fire, can see, know, save, or even recognize its own foolishness, while the LORD blots out sin and calls His people to return to Him.
section.frameNote: Six movements: the LORD's tender assurance and promised outpouring of the Spirit; His declaration as the first and the last, the only Rock; the folly of idol makers exposed; the extended satire of the blacksmith and the woodworker; the idol worshiper's blinded, deluded heart; and the closing call to remember and return, since the LORD has already redeemed Jacob.

units.label: Do not fear, O Jacob My servant

verses.text: But now listen, O Jacob My servant, Israel, whom I have chosen.
This is the word of the LORD, your Maker, who formed you from the womb and who will help you: “Do not be afraid, O Jacob My servant, Jeshurun, whom I have chosen.
For I will pour water on the thirsty land, and currents on the dry ground. I will pour out My Spirit on your descendants, and My blessing on your offspring.
They will sprout among the grass like willows by flowing streams.
One will say, ‘I belong to the LORD,’ another will call himself by the name of Jacob, and still another will write on his hand, ‘The LORD's,’ and will take the name of Israel.”

units.label: I am the first and the last

verses.text: Thus says the LORD, the King and Redeemer of Israel, the LORD of Hosts: “I am the first and I am the last, and there is no God but Me.
Who then is like Me? Let him say so! Let him declare his case before Me, since I established an ancient people. Let him foretell the things to come, and what is to take place.
Do not tremble or fear. Have I not told you and declared it long ago? You are My witnesses! Is there any God but Me? There is no other {{tsur|Rock}}; I know not one.”
All makers of idols are nothing, and the things they treasure are worthless. Their witnesses fail to see or comprehend, so they are put to shame.
Who fashions a god or casts an idol which profits him nothing?
Behold, all his companions will be put to shame, for the craftsmen themselves are only human. Let them all assemble and take their stand; they will all be brought to terror and shame.

units.label: The blacksmith and the woodworker
units.note: half for the fire, half for a god

verses.text: The blacksmith takes a tool and labors over the coals; he fashions an idol with hammers and forges it with his strong arms. Yet he grows hungry and loses his strength; he fails to drink water and grows faint.

The woodworker extends a measuring line; he marks it out with a stylus; he shapes it with chisels and outlines it with a compass. He fashions it in the likeness of man, like man in all his glory, that it may dwell in a shrine.
He cuts down cedars or retrieves a cypress or oak. He lets it grow strong among the trees of the forest. He plants a laurel, and the rain makes it grow.
It serves as fuel for man. He takes some of it to warm himself, and he kindles a fire and bakes his bread; he even fashions it into a god and worships it; he makes an idol and bows down to it.
He burns half of it in the fire, and he roasts meat on that half. He eats the roast and is satisfied. Indeed, he warms himself and says, “Ah! I am warm; I see the fire.”
From the rest he makes a god, his graven image. He bows down to it and worships; he prays to it and says, “Save me, for you are my god.”

units.label: Blinded eyes, deluded hearts
units.note: is this not a lie in my hand

verses.text: They do not comprehend or discern, for He has shut their eyes so they cannot see and closed their minds so they cannot understand.

And no one considers in his heart, no one has the knowledge or insight to say, “I burned half of it in the fire, and I baked bread on its coals; I roasted meat and I ate. Shall I make something detestable with the rest of it? Shall I bow down to a block of wood?”
He feeds on ashes. His {{sakal|deluded}} heart has led him astray, and he cannot deliver himself or say, “Is not this thing in my right hand a lie?”

units.label: Return to Me, I have redeemed you
units.note: sins blotted out like a cloud

verses.text: Remember these things, O Jacob, for you are My servant, O Israel. I have made you, and you are My servant; O Israel, I will never forget you.
I have blotted out your transgressions like a cloud, and your sins like a mist. Return to Me, for I have redeemed you.

Sing for joy, O heavens, for the LORD has done this; shout aloud, O depths of the earth. Break forth in song, O mountains, you forests and all your trees. For the LORD has redeemed Jacob, and revealed His glory in Israel.

tsur.gloss: Rock, a title for God emphasizing His unshakeable stability, permanence, and reliability as a foundation and refuge. Isaiah's declaration that there is no other Rock deliberately contrasts the LORD's solid, self-existent reality with idols carved from ordinary wood, a foundation no craftsman shaped and no fire can consume.

sakal.gloss: Used here in a form describing being deluded, led astray, or foolish, the opposite of true wisdom and discernment. The idol worshiper's sakal, deluded, heart cannot even ask the one obvious question, is this thing in my hand a lie, showing that idolatry does not merely lack wisdom but actively blinds the mind against it.

basic.context: This chapter moves from the LORD's tender promise to pour out His Spirit on Israel's descendants to one of the Bible's most pointed satires against idol worship: a craftsman uses half a log to warm himself and cook his dinner and the other half to carve a god he then bows down to and begs for salvation. The chapter closes with pure celebration, since the LORD has already blotted out Jacob's sin like a cloud that simply vanishes.
basic.hermeneutics: The idol-making satire is not exaggeration for its own sake; it exposes the built-in self-contradiction of idolatry, that the very hands shaping a supposed god are stronger and more real than the object they create. The chapter's central theological claim, that the LORD alone is the first and the last, the only Rock, sets up the naming of Cyrus in the very next section as proof that only the true God can declare the future before it happens.
basic.originalLanguages: The LORD declares there is no other {{tsur}}, Rock, a title emphasizing unshakeable permanence, in stark contrast to an idol carved from the same tree trunk used for firewood. The idol worshiper's heart is {{sakal}}, deluded or led astray, unable even to ask the plain question whether the object in his own hand might be a lie.
basic.typology: The promise to pour out the Spirit on Israel's descendants, verse 3, anticipates the fuller outpouring at Pentecost, Acts 2:17-18, when God's Spirit is given not merely to a nation but to all who call on the name of the Lord. The LORD's title, 'I am the first and I am the last,' verse 6, is claimed directly by the risen Christ, Revelation 1:17, 22:13.
basic.christ: The LORD's self-declaration, 'I am the first and I am the last, and there is no God but Me,' verse 6, is precisely the title Jesus applies to Himself as the risen Lord, 'I am the First and the Last,' Revelation 1:17, and 'the Alpha and the Omega, the First and the Last,' Revelation 22:13, identifying Christ fully with the eternal, incomparable God of this chapter. The promise that sins are 'blotted out like a cloud,' verse 22, anticipates the complete forgiveness secured at the cross, where sin is not merely covered but truly removed.
basic.god: God is the eternal Rock, incomparable to any rival, who alone declared the future before it happened and alone deserves worship. He also delights to pour out His own Spirit and blessing on ordinary descendants and to blot out sin completely, calling His wandering people simply to return, since He has already redeemed them.
basic.crossReferences: Revelation 1:17 and 22:13 apply 'the First and the Last' directly to the risen Christ. Acts 2:17-18 records the fuller outpouring of the Spirit at Pentecost anticipated in verse 3. Psalm 115:4-8 offers a similar satire of idols that cannot see, hear, or speak. Jeremiah 10:1-16 makes a parallel argument against the folly of idol worship.
basic.oneStory: This chapter's contrast between the powerless, self-made idol and the LORD who alone is Rock, Redeemer, and Spirit-giver runs through the whole of Scripture's insistence that salvation cannot be manufactured by human hands. It anticipates Christ, who claims the title 'the First and the Last' for Himself and completes the blotting out of sin this chapter only announces.

context.literary: Structured in six movements, moving from tender promise, through a courtroom-style declaration of God's incomparability, into an extended narrative satire of idol-making, and closing with joyful celebration over sin blotted out and redemption accomplished.
context.historical: Addressed to exiles in Babylon surrounded by the impressive idol worship of their captors, this chapter directly confronts the temptation to be drawn toward gods that seemed, by imperial standards, far more visibly successful than the God of a defeated, exiled people.
context.canonical: Continues the polemic against idolatry begun in chapters 40 to 41 and sets up the naming of Cyrus in the following section, chapters 44:24 to 45:25, as further proof that only the LORD, unlike any idol, can declare the future before it comes to pass.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To expose the self-contradiction of idol worship so thoroughly that no reader could mistake a carved image for a genuine rival to the LORD, and to assure God's people that the same LORD who alone is the first and the last has already redeemed them and blotted out their sin.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes and satirizes a specific ancient practice of idol manufacture; it is not merely an antique polemic but names a pattern, trusting the work of our own hands to save us, that recurs in every generation. Its abiding principle is that only God, never a created thing, can truly save, forgive, or declare the future.

debates.question: Is the extended satire of the blacksmith and woodworker, verses 12 to 17, a literal description of common ancient practice, or heightened rhetorical exaggeration?

views.label: A grounded description of real practice
views.case: Wood carving for idols and for fuel from the same available timber was a genuine practical reality in the ancient Near East, so the satire's power comes from its accuracy, not exaggeration.

views.label: Deliberate rhetorical satire for theological effect
views.case: Isaiah heightens the absurdity for maximum rhetorical impact, drawing out with almost comic detail the self-contradiction inherent in any idolatry, whether or not every craftsman used precisely half a log for each purpose.

debates.bearing: Either way, the passage's theological point stands undiminished: an object shaped by human hands from ordinary material cannot be a god, and trusting in one is self-evidently foolish once truly considered.

study.originalLanguages: The LORD is the only {{tsur}}, Rock, an image of unshakeable permanence set against wood shaped by a craftsman's own hands. Those who worship such an object have hearts described as {{sakal}}, deluded or led astray, unable even to ask themselves the obvious question whether what they hold is a lie.

typology.type: The Spirit poured out on Israel's descendants
typology.fulfillment: The fuller outpouring of the Holy Spirit at Pentecost on all who call on the Lord's name, Acts 2:17-18.

typology.type: The LORD as 'the first and the last'
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who applies this very title to Himself as the risen and eternal Lord, Revelation 1:17, 22:13.

study.christ: The LORD's declaration, 'I am the first and I am the last, and there is no God but Me,' verse 6, becomes the very title the risen Jesus claims for Himself, 'I am the First and the Last,' Revelation 1:17, and 'the Alpha and the Omega, the First and the Last, the Beginning and the End,' Revelation 22:13, identifying Christ fully with the eternal God who speaks in this chapter. The promise that sins are 'blotted out like a cloud,' verse 22, anticipates the complete and final forgiveness secured at the cross.
study.god: God alone is the eternal Rock, incomparable to any idol shaped by human hands, and He alone can truly declare the future because He alone governs it. He delights to pour out His Spirit and blessing on ordinary people and to blot out sin so completely that it vanishes like a cloud, calling His people simply to return to Him rather than earn their way back.

crossReferences.note: The risen Christ applies 'the First and the Last' to Himself, the same title the LORD claims in verse 6.
Records the fuller outpouring of the Spirit at Pentecost anticipated in verse 3's promise.
Offers a similar satirical critique of idols that cannot see, hear, or speak, though shaped by human hands.
Makes a parallel argument against idol worship, contrasting the living God with lifeless, man-made images.

study.oneStory: This chapter's contrast between a powerless, self-made idol and the LORD who alone is Rock, Redeemer, and Spirit-giver runs through the whole of Scripture's insistence that salvation cannot be manufactured by human hands. It anticipates Christ, who claims the title 'the First and the Last' for Himself and completes the blotting out of sin this chapter only begins to announce.

quiz.q: What does the LORD promise to pour out on Israel's descendants in verse 3?

options.text: His Spirit and His blessing.
options.note: Verse 3 states this promise directly, anticipating the later outpouring at Pentecost.

options.text: Military strength for conquest.
options.note: The promise given is the Spirit and blessing, not military might.

options.text: Wealth and material prosperity alone.
options.note: The specific promise is the Spirit and blessing, not merely material wealth.

options.text: A new set of written laws.
options.note: The promise concerns the Spirit and blessing poured out, not new legislation.

quiz.q: What title does the LORD claim exclusively for Himself in verses 6 to 8?

options.text: The first and the last, with no other Rock or God beside Him.
options.note: Verses 6 and 8 state this exclusive claim directly.

options.text: The greatest among many equally valid gods.
options.note: The opposite; the claim is exclusive, not merely superior among equals.

options.text: A god limited to ruling only over Israel's territory.
options.note: The claim made is universal and exclusive, not territorially limited.

options.text: A minor deity among a larger pantheon.
options.note: The claim made is total exclusivity as the only true God, not a minor role in a pantheon.

quiz.q: What absurd behavior does the extended satire of verses 12 to 17 describe?

options.text: A craftsman uses half a log to warm himself and cook food, and the other half to carve a god he worships and begs to save him.
options.note: Verses 15 to 17 describe exactly this self-contradictory behavior.

options.text: A craftsman carefully preserves an entire sacred tree without using any of it for ordinary purposes.
options.note: The opposite; the satire's point is that the very same wood is used for both fuel and the idol.

options.text: A craftsman refuses to use any wood at all for his idols.
options.note: The satire describes extensive wood use, both for the idol and ordinary fuel, not an avoidance of wood.

options.text: A craftsman sells his idols to foreign nations for profit.
options.note: The passage focuses on the craftsman's own worship of the object, not commercial sale to others.

quiz.q: Why can the idol worshiper not recognize his own folly, according to verses 18 to 20?

options.text: His eyes and mind have been shut so he cannot see, understand, or ask if what he holds is a lie.
options.note: These verses describe this spiritual blindness directly.

options.text: He has simply never seen an idol being made.
options.note: The text says he made the idol himself, using half the wood for cooking, so he witnessed the whole process.

options.text: He lacks access to any better religious teaching.
options.note: The passage attributes his blindness to a shut mind and deluded heart, not merely a lack of information.

options.text: He is secretly aware of the truth but chooses to lie to others.
options.note: The text describes genuine blindness and delusion, not conscious deception of others while secretly knowing the truth.

quiz.q: What has the LORD already done for Jacob according to verse 22?

options.text: Blotted out its transgressions like a cloud and its sins like a mist.
options.note: Verse 22 states this directly, describing complete removal of sin.

options.text: Permanently recorded its sins for future punishment.
options.note: The opposite; the image is of sins vanishing like a cloud, not being preserved for judgment.

options.text: Forgiven only a small portion of its sin.
options.note: The imagery of a cloud and mist vanishing conveys complete, not partial, removal.

options.text: Demanded further sacrifices before any forgiveness could occur.
options.note: The verse presents forgiveness as already accomplished, followed by a call to simply return, not further required sacrifice.

questions: The LORD promises to pour out His Spirit on ordinary descendants, not just leaders or priests. Where do you need to trust that God's Spirit is genuinely at work in your own ordinary life?

The idol maker uses half his wood for dinner and half for a god, without seeing the contradiction. What modern 'idols' do you use for both practical benefit and misplaced ultimate trust without noticing the contradiction?
The idol worshiper's heart was so deluded he could not even ask whether the thing in his hand was a lie. What belief or habit in your life might need that same honest question asked of it?

The LORD declares He has already blotted out Jacob's sin like a vanishing cloud, then simply calls the people to return. Is there a sin you are still trying to atone for yourself rather than simply returning to the God who has already forgiven it in Christ?

Jesus takes for Himself the title 'the First and the Last' claimed by the LORD in this chapter. How does recognizing Jesus's full identity with the eternal God change the weight you give His words and promises to you?

translationNotes.note: Jeshurun is a poetic, affectionate name for Israel, meaning roughly 'upright one,' used elsewhere only in Deuteronomy 32:15, 33:5, 26.
Writing the LORD's name on one's hand likely reflects an ancient practice of marking identity or ownership, here signifying deliberate, personal devotion to the LORD.
This extended satire against idol-making is one of the most detailed such passages in the Old Testament, paralleled in tone though not length by Psalm 115:4-8 and Jeremiah 10:1-16.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Isaiah 44:1-23. No em or en dashes appeared in the source text for this passage.
